---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: false
incremental: false
header-includes:
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
  \usepackage{tikz}
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric}
  \usetikzlibrary{overlay-beamer-styles}
---

```{=latex}
\title{{\fontsize{15pt}{16pt}\selectfont Applied Psychometrics}:\\ studying subclinical psychopathologies\\ \vspace{1.5em} \raggedright 
\emph{10.1 - Probability and clinical judgment}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}

\titlegraphic{
\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\begin{center}
\vspace{-2.2em}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
\end{center}
}
\date{2026}
\maketitle
```

## The real question
\centering
![](Figures/confused.png){width="70%"}

What is the probability of a clinical condition,\
given the score(s) at the tests?

## An Important Caveat
Sensitivity, specificity, and all the further indices discussed in these slides describe the behaviour of a test across a series of cases. They say nothing certain about the individual in front of you.


## How to think these lectures

![](Figures/mechanic_peek.png){width="90%" fig-align="center"}

## What a Test Score Actually Does
\underline{\textbf{A test result does not assign a probability to a specific person}}. It is a piece of evidence that shifts your assessment in one direction or another, toward or away from a clinical hypothesis.

Its weight depends on everything else you know about that person: history, interview, referral context, and prior clinical judgment.

\pause
\vfill
\textcolor{red}{The numbers contribute to the decision. \textbf{They do not make}}
